5.finding: Both the size and the number of eye facets increase as the mantis grows — quantifying how a compound eye scales with the body.
5.summary: Przibram and Brecher took the growth program to the mantis's eye, counting and measuring facets across instars to show how the compound eye enlarges and adds units as the animal grows. It is a precise piece of the BVA's quest for quantitative growth laws.

16.manipulation: Reared fire-salamander larvae against yellow versus black surroundings and scored the spot pattern of the resulting adults.
16.design: Adult spot pattern as a function of the larva's background colour.
16.readout: The black-and-yellow spot pattern of the adult salamander.
16.finding: The colour of the larva's surroundings influences the adult salamander's spot pattern — environmental determination of a 'fixed' colour pattern.
16.summary: Przibram and Dembowski reared salamander larvae on yellow or black backgrounds and found the adult's markings shifted accordingly, an environmental hold on a supposedly set pattern. A rediscovery target, it re-tested the kind of claim — environment writing onto pattern — that made Kammerer's salamander work famous and contested.

34.manipulation: Replanted the crystalline lens of developed (adult) animals and followed its fate.
34.design: Replantation of the lens with observation of survival and clarity.
34.readout: Whether the replanted lens survives and stays functional.
34.finding: The crystalline lens of adult animals can be replanted and survive — the lens, like the whole eye, tolerates transplantation.
34.summary: Wiesner showed that even the lens of a developed animal can be removed and re-set, extending the BVA's eye-transplantation line from the whole eye (Koppanyi) to its optical core. It probes how far a differentiated, avascular organ can be moved and still keep its job.

47.manipulation: Removed the anterior end of the polychaete worm Ophryotrocha puerilis and followed its regeneration.
47.readout: Rebuilding of the worm's anterior end.
47.finding: The worm regenerates its anterior end, including head structures, after amputation.
47.summary: Czwiklitzer documented anterior regeneration in the small polychaete Ophryotrocha, a worm that became a standard laboratory animal. A rediscovery target, it brought a tractable annelid into the BVA's regeneration atlas alongside Janda's Criodrilus.

52.manipulation: Removed the jaw in reptiles and amphibians and compared its regeneration across the two groups.
52.design: Comparative jaw regeneration in reptiles versus amphibians.
52.readout: Extent and structure of the regenerated jaw.
52.finding: Both reptiles and amphibians regenerate jaw structures, with differences in extent between the groups.
52.summary: Werber broadened his lizard-jaw study into a comparison of jaw regeneration across reptiles and amphibians, mapping how this capacity varies among cold-blooded vertebrates. It is part of his systematic survey of vertebrate regeneration for the institute.

63.manipulation: Measured the rates of the mantis's vital processes at different temperatures and computed their temperature coefficients.
63.design: Physiological rates across a temperature range, expressed as temperature quotients.
63.readout: The Q10 / temperature coefficients of life processes.
63.finding: Determines the temperature quotients for the mantis's vital processes — quantifying how strongly temperature scales physiology.
63.summary: Przibram measured how the mantis's life processes speed up with warmth and cast the result as temperature coefficients, bringing van 't Hoff's chemistry to whole-animal physiology. It is a clear expression of the BVA's drive to put biology on a quantitative, physico-chemical footing.

66.manipulation: Amputated the antennae of half-grown Sphodromantis mantis larvae and followed their regeneration.
66.design: Observation of antenna regeneration at a defined larval stage.
66.readout: Whether and how the antenna regrows, and in what form.
66.finding: Half-grown mantis larvae regenerate their antennae, with the regenerate's form depending on stage — relevant to the heteromorphosis question.
66.summary: Przibram tested antenna regeneration in half-grown mantis larvae, watching whether the regrowth is a true antenna or something else. A rediscovery target, it feeds the BVA's running analysis of when a regenerate keeps its identity and when it turns homeotic.

72.manipulation: Measured the angles of the venation / form of the butterfly wing.
72.readout: The characteristic angles of the butterfly wing.
72.finding: Quantifies the angular geometry of the butterfly wing, treating wing form as measurable morphometric data.
72.summary: Weiss brought precise measurement to the butterfly wing, recording its angles as hard morphometric data. The study reflects the BVA's conviction that even complex organic form should yield to number — the biometric habit Przibram instilled across the institute.

79.manipulation: Compared positive and negative DOPA reactions on frozen sections with those from extracts (companion communication).
79.design: Histochemical DOPA reaction in situ versus in extracts.
79.readout: Agreement between in-section and in-extract DOPA reactions.
79.finding: Confirms that the DOPA reaction matches between sections and extracts, validating the histochemical test for melanin-forming enzyme.
79.summary: A companion to their other DOPA-reaction paper, Brecher and Winkler again cross-checked the in-section and in-extract tests, securing the DOPA reaction as a reliable way to locate where melanin is made — a methodological anchor for the BVA's pigment chemistry.

1.manipulation: Mutilated the newt leg along its fibular edge and followed the regeneration.
1.readout: What regenerates from a leg cut back only along one edge.
1.finding: The newt regenerates structures after partial fibular-edge mutilation, probing how a partial wound is restored.
1.summary: Abolins followed how the newt rebuilds a leg mutilated only along its fibular edge, one of the late BVA Triton-limb studies that dissected regeneration edge by edge to map the limb's regenerative field.

2.manipulation: Inserted limbs into the urodele body and analysed the regeneration and symmetry of the resulting structures.
2.design: Regeneration and symmetry of limbs placed within the body.
2.readout: The symmetry and completeness of the regenerate.
2.finding: The symmetry of a regenerate depends on how the limb is set in the body — bearing on the rules of regenerative symmetry.
2.summary: Burchhardt studied regeneration and symmetry in limbs inserted through the urodele body, part of the institute's running analysis of what fixes the geometry of a regenerate — the symmetry rules Przibram framed as triple-formation.

6.manipulation: Mutilated newt legs along the tibial edge or medially, and noted a heteromorphosis of the long bone after excision of the fibular edge.
6.design: Regeneration across different edge-restricted mutilations, with attention to bone heteromorphosis.
6.readout: Regenerated structures and abnormal (heteromorphic) long-bone forms.
6.finding: Edge-restricted mutilations regenerate, and excision of the fibular edge can yield a heteromorphic long bone — a patterned abnormality.
6.summary: A jointly authored late-BVA paper extending the Triton edge-mutilation studies, reporting that cutting back specific edges of the leg can produce a heteromorphic long bone. It belongs to the institute's fine-grained mapping of the limb's regenerative field and its failure modes.

7.manipulation: Documented heteromorphoses at the spiracles and other structures of the stick insect Carausius (Dixippus) morosus.
7.design: Survey of heteromorphic regenerates at non-appendage sites.
7.readout: The aberrant structures formed at spiracles and elsewhere.
7.finding: Heteromorphoses arise even at spiracles and other unexpected sites in the stick insect, widening the catalogue of homeotic regeneration.
7.summary: Staudinger extended BVA heteromorphosis work beyond antennae and legs to the spiracles and other structures of the stick insect, showing how broadly a regenerate can take the wrong form. It enlarges the phenomenology behind Przibram's homeosis program.

13.manipulation: Lowered the body temperature of young rats by chemical means and measured the effect on tail length.
13.design: Tail length under chemically lowered body temperature versus normal.
13.readout: Tail length as a function of lowered body temperature.
13.finding: Lowering body temperature shortens the tail — the cold end of Przibram's temperature-controls-proportion series.
13.summary: Bierens de Haan and Przibram cooled young rats chemically and found shorter tails, the mirror image of their heat experiments. Together these papers built the BVA's experimental case that rearing temperature directly sets extremity length, an Allen's-rule mechanism.

15.manipulation: Observed the marking of the fire salamander under transmitted coloured light.
15.design: Salamander spot pattern under different transmitted light colours.
15.readout: The black-and-yellow pattern under coloured light.
15.finding: Transmitted coloured light affects how the salamander's pattern appears or develops, extending the background-and-light influence on its markings.
15.summary: Kammerer looked at the fire salamander's pattern under coloured light, a companion to the background-colour experiments on salamander markings. It is part of the contested BVA work on how environment shapes a 'fixed' colour pattern.

19.manipulation: Amputated and followed regeneration of the foreleg across various genera of mantids.
19.design: Comparative foreleg regeneration across mantid genera.
19.readout: Extent and pattern of foreleg regeneration.
19.finding: Documents how the raptorial foreleg regenerates across different mantids, a comparative extension of the BVA mantis work.
19.summary: Thomson's notes compared foreleg regeneration across mantid genera, broadening the institute's long study of the praying mantis from single species to a comparative survey of how the grasping leg rebuilds.

23.manipulation: Compiled the institute's English-language publications on quantitative biology from 1916 to 1919.
23.readout: A list of quantitative-biology output in English.
23.finding: Gathers the BVA's quantitative-biology papers published in English, documenting the program's international reach.
23.summary: This is a bibliographic notice rather than a research paper: Przibram listed the institute's English-language quantitative-biology publications of 1916-1919, a marker of how the BVA sought an international audience for its measurement-driven program.

26.manipulation: Removed and re-implanted eyes in fishes and followed their survival.
26.design: Replantation of the eye with observation of recovery.
26.readout: Survival and recovery of the replanted eye.
26.finding: Eyes can be replanted with survival, here in fishes.
26.summary: A fish instalment of Koppanyi's eye-replantation work, testing whether the removed and re-set eye survives across vertebrate groups — part of his systematic survey of where eye transplantation succeeds.

29.manipulation: Removed and re-implanted eyes in amphibians and followed their survival and function.
29.design: Replantation of the eye with observation of reconnection and recovery.
29.readout: Survival, healing and function of the replanted eye.
29.finding: The amphibian eye can be replanted and recover structure and some function.
29.summary: An amphibian instalment of Koppanyi's eye-replantation studies, the group where reconnection and recovery were most fully tested, feeding his later work on optic-nerve regeneration and vision.

30.manipulation: Re-implanted eyes in mammals and followed their fate.
30.design: Replantation of the eye in a mammal, with observation of survival.
30.readout: Whether the mammalian replanted eye survives.
30.finding: Tests how far eye replantation succeeds in mammals — the hardest case, with the least regenerative tolerance.
30.summary: Jellinek carried the BVA's eye-replantation question into mammals, where reconnection is most demanding. It rounds out the institute's cross-vertebrate test of how far a removed eye can be re-set and still function.

31.manipulation: Transplanted heads between insects (Lepidoptera) and observed survival and behaviour.
31.design: Head-exchange grafts with observation of the composite animal.
31.readout: Survival, behaviour and integration of the head-swapped insect.
31.finding: Reports that head-transplanted insects can survive for a time — Finkler's striking and much-debated claim.
31.summary: Finkler's head-transplantation experiments swapped insect heads and claimed the composite animals lived and behaved, a sensational result widely discussed and contested in the 1920s. It marks the bold, controversial edge of the BVA's transplantation program.

32.manipulation: Transplanted heads between beetles (Coleoptera) and followed the result.
32.readout: Survival and behaviour of the head-swapped beetle.
32.finding: Extends Finkler's head-transplant claim to beetles.
32.summary: A beetle instalment of Finkler's head-transplantation work, part of the contested series in which he reported surviving head-swapped insects — among the most provocative experiments associated with the institute.

33.manipulation: Transplanted insect heads and followed the composite animals (general communication).
33.readout: Survival and behaviour of head-swapped insects.
33.finding: Generalizes Finkler's claim that head-transplanted insects can survive and respond.
33.summary: The general statement of Finkler's insect head-transplantation experiments, gathering the much-debated claim that a swapped head can live on its new body — a high-water mark of experimental daring, and skepticism, around the BVA.

35.manipulation: Autotransplanted ovaries in rats and tested whether they remain functional.
35.design: Function of the autografted ovary versus normal.
35.readout: Whether the transplanted ovary maintains its endocrine and germinal function.
35.finding: Autoplastically transplanted ovaries remain functional — the female counterpart to Koppanyi's functional testis graft.
35.summary: Wiesner showed that a rat's ovary, grafted back into the same animal, keeps working, demonstrating gonadal autonomy under transplantation. With Koppanyi's testis and Steinach's gland work it built the BVA's case on how far a sex gland governs its own function.

45.manipulation: Amputated the third maxilliped of the crayfish and followed its regeneration.
45.readout: Extent and form of the regenerated maxilliped.
45.finding: The crayfish regenerates its third maxilliped, a specialized mouthpart-leg.
45.summary: Biberhofer documented regeneration of a specific crayfish appendage, the third maxilliped, adding a fine-grained crustacean case to the BVA's regeneration atlas alongside Przibram's claw studies.

46.manipulation: Tested the regenerative capacity of freshwater snails after removal of body parts.
46.finding: Maps the regenerative powers of freshwater snails.
46.summary: Cerny surveyed regeneration in freshwater snails, an early mollusc entry in the BVA's comparative regeneration program that Czerny and Megusar would also pursue across gastropods.

53.manipulation: Tested the regenerative capacity of the lancelet Amphioxus lanceolatus after amputation.
53.design: Observation of regeneration in a basal chordate.
53.finding: Probes regeneration in the lancelet, a basal chordate of special phylogenetic interest.
53.summary: Biberhofer tested regeneration in Amphioxus, the cephalochordate that sits near the base of the chordates. Its place in vertebrate ancestry made the question of its regenerative power especially pointed for the institute's comparative survey.

58.manipulation: Subjected water-beetle embryos to abnormal gravitational conditions and observed their development.
58.design: Embryonic development under abnormal gravity versus normal.
58.readout: Effects of altered gravity on the embryo.
58.finding: Abnormal gravity disturbs the beetle's embryonic development, probing how far gravity is a developmental cue.
58.summary: Megusar manipulated gravity acting on water-beetle eggs to ask whether the embryo's development depends on its gravitational orientation, a classic Entwicklungsmechanik question (after Roux and Pfluger) brought into the BVA's insect work.

61.manipulation: Took further serial growth measurements of the mantis Sphodromantis bioculata.
61.design: Longitudinal biometry of mantis growth across instars.
61.finding: Adds to the quantitative growth curve of the mantis across its development.
61.summary: One of Przibram's recurring growth-measurement communications on the mantis, accumulating the instar-by-instar data behind the BVA's search for quantitative growth laws in its flagship insect.

68.manipulation: Commemorates the life and work of the BVA researcher Dr. Franz Megusar.
68.readout: An account of Megusar's career and contributions.
68.finding: Records the death and scientific legacy of Franz Megusar, who worked on insect and mollusc regeneration at the institute.
68.summary: This is an obituary, not a research paper: Przibram's memorial for Franz Megusar, the BVA worker behind the beetle-, orthopteran- and snail-regeneration studies. It is included for completeness as part of the corpus.

75.manipulation: Measured rat tail length and analysed it as a facultative sex character.
75.design: Tail length compared between the sexes (and against temperature).
75.finding: Tail length differs between the sexes, behaving as a facultative (conditional) sex character.
75.summary: Przibram read rat tail length as a 'facultative sex character', tying his temperature-and-tail program to sex differences in proportion. It is part of the cluster of rat-tail papers that made the tail a model trait for environment-and-sex effects on form.

76.manipulation: Bred temperature-modified rats and measured the tail length of their offspring.
76.design: Offspring tail length of temperature-modified parents versus controls.
76.readout: Whether the parents' temperature-induced tail change appears in offspring.
76.finding: Tests whether a temperature-induced change in tail length is transmitted to the offspring — a direct Lamarckian test.
76.summary: Przibram pushed the rat-tail work into heredity, asking whether tails lengthened or shortened by temperature stay changed in the next generation. It is the inheritance test at the heart of the BVA's contested environment-writes-heredity claims.

77.manipulation: Measured how the relative tail length increases and computed its temperature coefficient in rats.
77.design: Relative tail-length growth across temperatures, expressed as a temperature quotient.
77.readout: Relative tail length and its Q10.
77.finding: Relative tail length grows with a measurable temperature coefficient — putting a number on how strongly temperature scales extremity growth.
77.summary: Przibram quantified the temperature dependence of relative tail length as a coefficient, the most precise statement of his rule that warmth lengthens extremities. It fuses the rat-tail program with his temperature-quotient approach to physiology.

80.manipulation: Determined when DOPA appears during the formation of the cocoons of the pine sawfly Lophyrus pini.
80.design: Timing of the DOPA (melanin-precursor) reaction across cocoon formation.
80.readout: The developmental moment at which DOPA is present.
80.finding: Pinpoints when DOPA, the melanin precursor, forms during cocoon construction.
80.summary: Sciacchitano timed the appearance of DOPA in sawfly cocoons, extending the BVA's tyrosinase/DOPA melanin program (Przibram, Brecher, Winkler) to a precise developmental schedule for when the pigment chemistry switches on.

87.manipulation: Compared the chamber progression of foraminifera with the moulting progression of mantids as parallel growth series.
87.design: Analysis of two growth series under a common geometric law.
87.readout: The progression (ratio series) of foraminiferan chambers and mantid moults.
87.finding: Foraminiferan shell-chamber growth follows a progression analogous to the mantid moulting series — a shared quantitative growth law across distant taxa.
87.summary: Przibram set the spiral chamber-addition of foraminifera beside the stepwise moulting of mantids, arguing both obey the same kind of growth progression. It is a characteristic BVA attempt to find one quantitative law of growth running across the animal kingdom.

89.manipulation: Contributed to the question of whether and how the environment reaches the germ-plasm.
89.design: Analysis within the institute's germ-plasm-and-environment series.
89.readout: Whether the surroundings can affect the hereditary substance.
89.finding: Argues, with the BVA line, that the germ-plasm is not sealed off from its environment.
89.summary: Walther's 'Umwelt des Keimplasmas' is one of several institute contributions under that title, pressing the shared claim that environment can reach the hereditary substance. The cluster of same-titled papers shows how central the germ-plasm-and-environment question was to the BVA.

90.manipulation: Crossed Japanese waltzing mice with European mice and followed the inheritance of their traits.
90.design: Breeding crosses analysed for trait transmission (notably the waltzing behaviour).
90.readout: Phenotypes and behaviour of the offspring.
90.finding: Analyses how traits, including the waltzing character, are inherited in mouse crosses.
90.summary: Hammerschlag used the Japanese waltzing mouse, a classic genetic curiosity, in crosses with European mice to study inheritance of its distinctive behaviour. It places the BVA inside the Mendelian-era mouse-genetics literature.

95.manipulation: Contributed experimental material to the question of the environment's reach into the germ-plasm.
95.design: Part of the institute's germ-plasm-and-environment series (amphibian material).
95.readout: Evidence on whether surroundings modify the germ-plasm.
95.finding: Adds amphibian evidence to the BVA case that environment can influence the germ-plasm.
95.summary: Secerov's contribution under the 'Umwelt des Keimplasmas' banner brought amphibian material to the institute's germ-plasm-and-environment debate, one of the cluster of same-titled papers arguing the hereditary substance is open to its surroundings.

101.manipulation: Amputated the antenna of the woodlouse Porcellio scaber and followed its regeneration.
101.readout: Extent and form of the regenerated antenna.
101.finding: The terrestrial isopod regenerates its antenna.
101.summary: Klintz documented antenna regeneration in the common woodlouse, adding a terrestrial isopod to the BVA's broad crustacean regeneration survey.

102.manipulation: Measured the heat-coagulation point of specific muscle plasmas and tested its dependence on the animal's living temperature.
102.design: Coagulation temperature of muscle proteins from animals living at different temperatures.
102.readout: The coagulation point of muscle plasma.
102.finding: The coagulation point of muscle plasma is independent of the temperature the animal lived at — a fixed biochemical property.
102.summary: Kryz tested whether a cold- or warm-living fish has muscle proteins that coagulate at correspondingly different temperatures, and found the coagulation point fixed. The physico-chemical result fits the BVA's effort to ground physiology in measurable protein chemistry.

103.manipulation: Gradually transferred freshwater fishes to salt water to test their adaptation.
103.design: Survival and condition across increasing salinity.
103.readout: Tolerance and adaptation to raised salinity.
103.finding: Freshwater fishes can be adapted to salt water within limits — experimental osmotic acclimation.
103.summary: Neudorfer tested how far freshwater fish can be pushed toward sea-water salinity, an experimental probe of osmotic tolerance and adaptation. It complements Zuelzer's contractile-vacuole osmoregulation work in the BVA's environmental-physiology line.

104.manipulation: Removed hermit crabs from their shells and observed how the abdomen differentiates.
104.design: Abdominal form of de-shelled versus shelled hermit crabs.
104.readout: Differentiation and form of the (normally asymmetric, soft) abdomen.
104.finding: The hermit crab's abdomen differentiates in response to the presence or absence of its shell — external conditions shape the soft body.
104.summary: Przibram took hermit crabs out of their shells to see whether their asymmetric, shell-fitted abdomen is fixed or responsive, finding that the abdomen's form depends on the shell. It is a neat case of the environment moulding body form, central to his program.

105.manipulation: Observed the automatic shedding (autotomy) of malformed regenerates in arthropods.
105.design: Survey of how defective regenerates are cast off.
105.readout: Whether and when a malformed regenerate is autotomized.
105.finding: Arthropods automatically shed malformed regenerates — a self-correcting autotomy that clears defective regrowth.
105.summary: Przibram described how arthropods cast off badly formed regenerates and start again, a kind of quality control in regeneration. It connects autotomy, regeneration and the heteromorphosis problem he studied throughout.

113.manipulation: Enforced colour changes and bred the animals to test whether the changes are inherited (reptile material).
113.design: Offspring of colour-modified animals versus controls.
113.readout: Whether the enforced colour change reappears in offspring.
113.finding: Kammerer reported inheritance of experimentally enforced colour changes (a neo-Lamarckian claim, later disputed).
113.summary: An earlier statement of Kammerer's enforced-colour-inheritance claim, the reptile-flavoured companion to his salamander colour and form papers. It belongs to the contested neo-Lamarckian work at the centre of the modern epigenetics-revival debate.

118.manipulation: Removed the front and hind ends of the leech Clepsine tessulata and followed their regeneration.
118.design: Regeneration of anterior versus posterior ends.
118.readout: Rebuilding of head and tail ends.
118.finding: The leech regenerates both its anterior and posterior ends, with differences in capacity between them.
118.summary: Gluschkiewitsch tested head- and tail-end regeneration in a leech, probing the front-back polarity of annelid regeneration. It joins Janda's and Czwiklitzer's worm studies in the BVA's annelid regeneration line.

120.manipulation: Described the symbiosis between dragonfly larvae and a filamentous alga.
120.design: Observational study of the symbiotic association.
120.readout: The nature and effects of the symbiosis.
120.finding: Documents a symbiotic association between dragonfly larvae and filamentous algae.
120.summary: Kammerer described a symbiosis between dragonfly larvae and algae, a more ecological note among the BVA's experimental papers. It reflects the institute's broad biological curiosity beyond manipulation and measurement.

121.manipulation: Removed and followed the regeneration of secondary sexual characters in amphibians.
121.design: Regeneration of secondary sex characters (e.g., breeding crest, thumb pads).
121.readout: Whether and how the secondary sex characters regrow.
121.finding: Amphibians regenerate their secondary sexual characters, linking regeneration to the sex-character (hormonal) system.
121.summary: Kammerer showed amphibians rebuilding their secondary sexual characters after removal, bringing regeneration and the gonad-controlled sex characters together. It anticipates the BVA's later endocrine work (Steinach, Brescia) on what governs these traits.

122.manipulation: Tested the regenerative capacity of cyclopoid copepods after injury.
122.finding: Cyclopoid copepods show only weak regenerative capacity — an informative negative case.
122.summary: Klintz documented the poor regeneration of cyclopoid copepods, a limit case that mattered as much as the positive ones for mapping where in the animal kingdom regenerative power runs strong or weak.

123.manipulation: Removed the tentacle and eye of the pond snail Limnaea stagnalis and followed their regeneration.
123.design: Observation of tentacle and eye regeneration.
123.readout: Rebuilding of the tentacle and its eye.
123.finding: The pond snail regenerates its tentacle together with the eye it carries.
123.summary: Megusar showed the pond snail rebuilding its eye-bearing tentacle, a striking case of sense-organ regeneration in a mollusc. It extends the BVA's regeneration survey into gastropods and to the eye specifically.

124.manipulation: Removed the caudal horn of the silkworm caterpillar Bombyx mori and followed its regeneration.
124.readout: Whether and how the caudal horn regrows.
124.finding: The silkworm caterpillar regenerates its caudal horn.
124.summary: Megusar tested regeneration of the silkworm's tail horn, a defined larval structure in an economically familiar insect. It is one more body part in the institute's comprehensive catalogue of what insects can regrow.

126.manipulation: Removed lung tissue in the fire salamander and other amphibians and followed its regeneration.
126.design: Observation of lung regeneration across amphibians.
126.readout: Whether and how lung tissue regrows.
126.finding: Amphibians regenerate lung tissue — regeneration of an internal organ, not only external appendages.
126.summary: Muftic carried the BVA's regeneration question inside the body, to the amphibian lung, showing that visceral organs as well as limbs can regrow. Internal-organ regeneration broadened the institute's mostly appendage-focused survey.

136.manipulation: Contributed further amphibian material to the question of the environment's reach into the germ-plasm.
136.design: Part of the germ-plasm-and-environment series, with a sex-determination angle.
136.readout: Evidence on environmental influence on the germ-plasm.
136.finding: Adds further evidence to the BVA claim that surroundings can act on the germ-plasm, with attention to sex determination.
136.summary: A second Secerov contribution under 'Umwelt des Keimplasmas', bringing sex-determination considerations into the institute's germ-plasm-and-environment debate. It is one of the cluster of same-titled papers that made the question a BVA signature.

137.manipulation: Tested experimental tail regeneration in dormice and other mammals.
137.design: Observation of mammalian tail regeneration after amputation.
137.finding: Probes how far mammals regenerate the tail — testing the limited regenerative powers of warm-blooded vertebrates.
137.summary: Klintz asked whether dormice and other mammals can regenerate a lost tail, testing the upper, least-regenerative reaches of the vertebrate scale. The mostly limited results helped fix where strong regeneration ends in the animal kingdom.

139.manipulation: Measured whether the size of freshly hatched mantises increases with the mother's age.
139.design: Hatchling size as a function of maternal age (a test of Halban's maternal-age effect).
139.readout: Size of newly hatched mantises across maternal ages.
139.finding: Freshly hatched mantises do NOT increase in size with the mother's age — a negative result for the maternal-age effect in this species.
139.summary: Przibram and Walther tested Halban's claim that older mothers make larger offspring, now in the mantis, and found no such effect on hatchling size. The clean negative shows the BVA holding its quantitative claims to species-by-species checking.

142.manipulation: Reared the pupae of Vanessid butterflies and recorded their colour.
142.design: Light and background treatment series on Vanessid pupae.
142.readout: Pupal colour versus light and background.
142.finding: Vanessid pupae adapt their colour to light and background, as in the cabbage white.
142.summary: A further instalment of Brecher's Vanessid pupal-colour work, confirming environmentally cued colour adaptation across the tortoiseshell and peacock butterflies and reinforcing that the Pieris phenomenon is general.

144.manipulation: Transplanted the spleen in adult urodeles and followed the graft.
144.design: Observation of spleen-graft survival and behaviour.
144.readout: Survival and condition of the transplanted spleen.
144.finding: Tests whether a visceral organ, the spleen, can be transplanted and survive in adult newts.
144.summary: Ehrenpreis extended the BVA's transplantation work from eyes, limbs and gonads to the spleen, a visceral organ, in adult newts. It probes how broadly organs tolerate grafting within the institute's favoured urodeles.

151.manipulation: Studied the biology and husbandry of the Iberian ribbed newt Pleurodeles waltl as an experimental animal.
151.design: Observational study establishing the species for laboratory work.
151.readout: Life habits, rearing and biology of Pleurodeles.
151.finding: Characterizes the biology of Pleurodeles, helping establish it as a tractable experimental newt.
151.summary: Koppanyi worked out the biology and husbandry of the ribbed newt Pleurodeles, the kind of foundational organism-building the BVA did for its mantids and salamanders. Pleurodeles would go on to become a standard amphibian model in developmental biology.

152.manipulation: Raised the body temperature of young rats above normal and measured the effect on tail length.
152.design: Tail length under raised body temperature versus normal.
152.readout: Tail length as a function of raised body temperature.
152.finding: Raising body temperature lengthens the tail — the heat counterpart to the chemical-cooling experiment, confirming temperature sets extremity length.
152.summary: Przibram and Wiesner warmed young rats above normal and measured longer tails, the heat arm of the temperature-and-tail series. Paired with the cooling experiments, it nailed down the direct, two-way dependence of tail length on rearing temperature.

155.manipulation: Examined leg-segments (tarsal joints) appearing on beetle antennae as heteromorphic regenerates.
155.design: Analysis of leg-for-antenna substitution in beetles.
155.finding: Beetles can regenerate leg-segments in place of antennal parts — a homeotic heteromorphosis.
155.summary: Przibram's note on 'leg-segments on beetle antennae' is another case in his homeosis program, where a regenerate takes the identity of the wrong appendage. With Janda's eye-to-antenna and Brecher's antenna-foot work it built the BVA's homeotic catalogue.

162.manipulation: Described the spontaneous formation of a second lens (lentoid) in the eye of a catfish.
162.design: Histological description of a developmental anomaly.
162.readout: Structure and position of the spontaneous second lens.
162.finding: Documents a spontaneously formed second lens in a fish eye — a natural lentoid bearing on lens formation and regeneration.
162.summary: Kolmer reported a catfish that had grown a second lens on its own, a natural experiment in lens formation. It complements Uhlenhuth's and Wiesner's induced lens-regeneration work by showing the eye can make a lentoid spontaneously.

163.manipulation: Discussed the question of whether characters acquired by the soma can be inherited.
163.readout: Arguments for and against the inheritance of somatically acquired characters.
163.finding: Weighs the case for and against the inheritance of acquired characters — the central theoretical question behind the BVA's Lamarckian experiments.
163.summary: Demoll's paper is a critical discussion of whether the soma can pass acquired characters to the germ line, the theoretical battlefield on which Kammerer's and Przibram's experiments were fought. It situates the institute's empirical claims within the wider Lamarckism debate.

166.manipulation: Described the skeleton of a partial hermaphrodite (gynandromorph).
166.design: Anatomical description of an intersex specimen.
166.readout: Skeletal structure of the partial hermaphrodite.
166.finding: Documents the skeletal anatomy of a partial hermaphrodite, a natural intersex of interest to the sex-determination question.
166.summary: Stieve described the skeleton of a partial hermaphrodite, a natural intersex specimen that spoke to the BVA's strong interest in sex determination and the sex glands (Steinach, Wiesner). The anatomy of such mixed individuals helped frame what 'sex' means structurally.

173.manipulation: Tested whether the female cycle and pregnancy of rats depend on temperature.
173.design: Female reproductive cycle and gestation across temperatures.
173.readout: Timing of the cycle and pregnancy under varied temperature.
173.finding: The female cycle and pregnancy are independent of temperature — a negative result contrasting with the temperature-dependent tail length.
173.summary: Przibram showed that, unlike tail length, the rat's reproductive cycle and gestation do not shift with temperature. The contrast sharpened his picture of which traits temperature can mould and which it cannot, a key calibration of the BVA's environmental program.

175.manipulation: Argued, in a short general-morphology essay, for a relation between organic form and growth velocity.
175.readout: The proposed link between form and rate of growth.
175.finding: Frames form as a product of growth velocity — a programmatic statement of the BVA's rate-based view of morphology.
175.summary: A brief essay in Die Naturwissenschaften, 'Form and Velocity' states Przibram's general thesis that an organism's form follows from its rates of growth. More manifesto than experiment, it distills the institute's conviction that morphology is ultimately a quantitative, dynamic problem.
